Phone Smashed and Photos Gone? Please Turn On Google Photos Backup (iPhone Setup Guide)

Phone smashed, dropped in water, suddenly won't boot — when it happens, the photos are usually the hardest thing to recover. This post walks through why I keep recommending Google Photos backup, and shows the full iPhone setup flow: choosing backup quality, how to use the 15GB free quota, whether to enable mobile data backup, and the one big iOS gotcha — swiping the app away in the App Switcher kills the upload mid-transfer.
What Are Claude Code's Built-in Tools Doing? Understand How It Picks Tools — Then Write Better Prompts

This post is a quick chat about Claude Code's built-in Tools. If you've used Claude Code, you've probably noticed something — sometimes when you ask it to read a file, it just opens it with Read. Other times when you ask "what components does this project have," it goes off and runs Glob and Grep. And occasionally, it takes a detour through Bash's cat or grep, which is confusing because the built-in tools are right there. Claude actually has its own logic for picking tools — this post walks through what built-in Tools Claude Code has, how it decides which one to use, and how to reverse-engineer its tool usage to fix your prompts.
Want to Start Vibe Coding? The Fundamentals Beginners Should Get Down First

Lately a lot of friends have been asking me, "I want to use AI to build things too — where do I start?" And what they're really asking about is Vibe Coding. This post is about the fundamentals beginners should get down before diving into Vibe Coding — Git, command line, UI Frameworks, and so on. So you don't get halfway through and find you're stuck not knowing how to communicate with the AI.
Decoding AI Spec Sheets: Token, Context Window, Compute — What Are These Numbers Actually About?

Every time you check out an AI product's intro page, you see things like Token, Context Window, hundreds of billions of parameters, $X per million Tokens — but what do these numbers actually mean? This post breaks down Token, Context Window, compute, parameters, Temperature, and all the other jargon AI vendors love to use, so you don't get lost in marketing numbers anymore.
How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl

If you've just started using a Mac for development, you've probably noticed there are several different ways to install the same tool — download a `.dmg` from the official site, install with Homebrew, run a `curl` install script, and tutorials sometimes recommend different approaches for the same tool. This post sorts through the common Mac install methods, pairs them with real examples (Git, Node.js, Python, VS Code), so you know which one to pick when.
From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready's AI Agent Readiness Test

I ran my blog through Cloudflare's isitagentready.com and got a brutal 3/100. Here's how I fixed every single item, one by one, all the way to a perfect score — code, screenshots, and the gotchas I hit along the way.
Migrating from Firebase Storage to Cloudflare R2 — the Full Move

Earlier I wrote about caching Firebase Storage images with Cloudflare Workers, but in the end caching is just a layer in front — the images still live on Firebase and the bill still runs. So I ended up moving everything off the two Firebase Storage buckets onto Cloudflare R2 — nearly 4800 images, 2200+ article links handled in one shot. This is the full migration log, including the gotchas and the final architecture.
Cache Firebase Storage Images with Cloudflare Workers — Completely Free Bandwidth Savings

If you're using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you've probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up — every article view pulls the images straight from Firebase Storage, and all of it lands on your Firebase bill. This post walks through how to use Cloudflare Workers plus a custom subdomain to cache those images. The whole setup is free and doesn't require a credit card.
Want to Run Your Own LLM? Understanding Model Parameters and Quantization

If you've ever tried downloading an AI model to run on your own machine, you've probably hit Hugging Face and seen 7B, Q4_K_M, GGUF — a wall of codes with no clue which one your computer can handle. This post unpacks what those codes actually mean and how to pick a model that fits your hardware.
AI Agent Isn't One Thing Anymore — Understanding Coding Agents and Other AI Agent Types

Back when I wrote about AI terminology, AI Agent was used as a catch-all term for systems that can understand a task on their own, break it into steps, and use tools to get things done. But here in 2026, the term AI Agent has started to splinter into more specific subcategories — and the most mature one is the Coding Agent (some people call it Code Agent). This post walks through what these categories actually mean and how they differ.
